cards.back: The preprocessor is a program that processes source code before compilation, handling directives that start with #.

cards.text: Preprocessor directives start with a {{c1::#}} symbol and are processed {{c2::before}} compilation.

cards.back: #include replaces the directive with the contents of the specified file, effectively copying that file's contents into the current file.

cards.text: #include {{c1::<iostream>}} uses angle brackets for {{c2::standard library}} headers. #include {{c3::"myheader.h"}} uses quotes for {{c4::user-defined}} headers.

cards.back: A macro is a preprocessor directive that defines a text substitution. When the preprocessor encounters the macro name, it replaces it with the defined text.

cards.text: Macros are defined using {{c1::#define}} followed by the macro name and replacement text.

cards.question: What does this code become after preprocessing?
cards.explanation: The preprocessor replaces MAX_VALUE with 100 before the code is compiled.

cards.back: Directives like #ifdef, #ifndef, #if, #else, #endif that allow including or excluding code based on whether certain macros are defined.

cards.question: What does this preprocessor code do?
cards.answer: The cout statement is only included in compilation if DEBUG is defined.
cards.explanation: #ifdef checks if a macro is defined. Code between #ifdef and #endif is conditionally included.

cards.front: Why should you prefer const variables over #define macros for constants?
cards.back:
const variables:
  • Have type safety
  • Respect scope rules
  • Show up in debugger
  • Provide better error messages
#define is simple text replacement with no type checking.

cards.explanation:
Main preprocessor directives:
  • #include - Include header files
  • #define - Define macros/constants
  • #ifdef/#ifndef - Conditional compilation
  • #if/#else/#endif - More complex conditionals
  • #pragma - Compiler-specific directives
